name: Pico de Miravalles y Sierra de Mijedo – running circular by the Camino del Norte
name_original: Pico de Miravalles y Sierra de Mijedo – running circular por el Camino del Norte
region_intro: Jogging routes around Noja, a coastal town in Cantabria, offer diverse landscapes for runners. The region features extensive sandy beaches, such as Playa de Trengandín and Ris Beach, providing flat terrain for runs. Coastal paths wind along the shoreline, offering varied surfaces and views of the Cantabrian Sea. Inland, protected wetlands like the Marismas de Victoria y Joyel and green hills present opportunities for more challenging runs with elevation. Best jogging routes around Noja The most popular jogging route is Pico de Miravalles y Sierra de Mijedo – running circular by the Camino del Norte, a 4.2 miles (6.7 km) trail that takes 53 minutes to complete. This route offers a circular path through the hills, providing panoramic views of the surrounding area. Another top favourite among local runners is Playa de Isla – View of Noja and the coast loop from Noja, a moderate 8.8 miles (14.2 km) path. This route provides extensive views of the coastline and the town of Noja. Local runners also love the Church with Clock Tower – Stone Wall with Wooden Stump loop from Noja, a 2.9 miles (4.7 km) trail leading through the town's surroundings, often completed in about 29 minutes. Jogging around Noja is defined by extensive sandy beaches, scenic coastal paths, and green hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy coastal runs to more strenuous hill climbs. The routes in Noja are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 800 runners have used komoot to explore Noja's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Noja?
A: Noja offers a wide variety of running opportunities, with over 40 routes available for you to explore. These range from easy coastal paths to more challenging hill climbs.

Q: Are there any easy running trails suitable for beginners or a relaxed jog?
A: Yes, Noja has several easy running trails. For a gentle run, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33926755" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church with Clock Tower – Stone Wall with Wooden Stump loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt;, which is just under 3 miles (4.7 km) and takes about 29 minutes to complete. The extensive sandy beaches like Playa de Trengandín also offer flat, easy terrain for a relaxed jog.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in Noja?
A: Absolutely. Many of Noja's running paths are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e999317429/pico-de-miravalles-y-sierra-de-mijedo-running-circular-by-the-camino-del-norte" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de Miravalles y Sierra de Mijedo – running circular by the Camino del Norte&lt;/a&gt;, offering panoramic views over 4.2 miles (6.7 km).

Q: What kind of scenery can I expect on the running trails in Noja?
A: Noja's running trails offer incredibly diverse scenery. You can jog along extensive sandy beaches like Playa de Trengandín and Ris Beach, follow dramatic coastal paths with ocean views, or venture into green hills and mountains such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3283461" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Miravalles Peak&lt;/a&gt; for impressive vistas. Some routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20513743" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bird Observatory loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt;, also provide opportunities for wildlife observation.

Q: Are there any running routes that are suitable for families?
A: For families, the flatter sections of the coastal paths and the wide, sandy beaches like Playa de Trengandín are excellent choices. These areas provide open space and generally easier terrain, making them enjoyable for all ages. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33926755" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church with Clock Tower – Stone Wall with Wooden Stump loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt; is also an easy, shorter option.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the running trails in Noja?
A: Many of the natural areas and coastal paths around Noja are dog-friendly, making them great for running with your canine companion. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in protected natural areas or during peak seasons on beaches, and to check local regulations for specific trails or beach access.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or points of interest I might see while running?
A: While running in Noja, you can encounter various natural and historical landmarks. The coastal routes offer views of dramatic rock formations and the vast ocean. Inland, you might pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3283461" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Miravalles Peak&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3387497" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Brusco (Mijedo)&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20513743" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bird Observatory loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt; is ideal for nature lovers, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33926755" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church with Clock Tower – Stone Wall with Wooden Stump loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt; takes you through the town's surroundings.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Noja?
A: The running routes in Noja are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 10 reviews. Runners often praise the diverse landscapes, from the extensive sandy beaches to the scenic coastal paths and green hills, offering varied terrain and beautiful views for all ability levels.

Q: Are there any running routes that offer views of the wider Noja coastline?
A: Yes, for panoramic views of the Noja coastline and beyond, consider routes that climb into the surrounding hills or follow clifftop paths. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24722785" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Playa de Isla – View of Noja and the coast loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt; is a great option, providing expansive vistas over 14.2 km (8.8 miles).

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Noja?
A: Noja is suitable for running year-round, but the spring and autumn months offer particularly pleasant temperatures and fewer crowds. Summer is also popular, especially for beach runs, though it can be warmer. In winter, the coastal paths and beaches remain accessible, offering a peaceful running experience with crisp air.

Q: Are there any running routes that are mostly paved or suitable for road running?
A: While many routes feature natural terrain, some, like parts of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20513743" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bird Observatory loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt;, follow mostly paved surfaces. This makes them suitable for road running shoes and offers a smoother experience, often allowing for observation of local wildlife and natural scenery.

Q: Where can I find parking near the running trails in Noja?
A: Noja, being a popular coastal town, generally offers various parking options, especially near its main beaches and town center. Many trailheads, particularly those starting from the town or popular beach access points, will have nearby public parking facilities. It's advisable to check local signage for specific parking regulations and availability.

Q: Are there any running routes that start from Isla Playa?
A: Yes, you can find running routes that start from the nearby area of Isla Playa. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33894320" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church with Clock Tower – Stone Wall with Wooden Stump loop from Isla Playa&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 5 km (3.1 miles) route that begins from there, offering a different starting point to explore the region.
tips: To climb the Miravalles peak you have to make a small and easy climb to find the mailbox, but once there the views of the Noja and Buciero area with Santoña at your feet are beautiful.

The National Geographic Institute and Google confuse this Cima del Mijedo and call it Brusco. Both the locals of the two slopes and the closest Institutions, Patronato de las Marismas, the Counselor for Rural Development, nautical charts and historical maps, call this summit as Cima Mijedo, and as such it is marked on the tablets inside the Mount. . The so-called Cueto Cabrero has always been called Brusco, where the Napoleonic ruins of Fort Brusco are located.
